sentences.text: My name is Ruston, Edward Walter Ruston.
Generally known as Ted though, my friends.
Yes.
R-U-S-T-O-N, Ruston.
I was born in the Fenland district of England in the United Kingdom, in a little town, little market town, called Chatteris, some 5,000 people.
And I was born in 1915, which makes me an octogenarian.
Yes, C-H-A-T-T-E-R-I-S.
It is some 20 miles north of Cambridge, and 20 miles southeast of Peterborough, both well-known cities in that area of England.
First of-- 1st of August, 1915.
81, rising 82.
Well, when I was born, the First World War was raging throughout Europe.
And it affected our family no less than everyone else.
The-- our wall memorial has got something like 200 names on it.
And with a population of 5,000, you can realize what an enormous bleeding of the human personnel in our little town the war caused.
When I was a boy at school, there were quite a number of boys whose fathers had been killed in the war.
It was all very sad.
The war was followed by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what can only be described as a deep recession for which the politicians had no answer.
They didn't really know what to do.
Germany, of course, was in total collapse.
And there was a lot of discontent, which I as a boy, could sense.
And yet, in the little village we had, we were very happy, very, very happy fraternity.
With only 5,000 people, everybody knew everybody else's business.
And that's perhaps for the better when it's bad news, because it is very quickly shared.
I was fortunate enough to go to a very, very good school as a boy.
Of course, I left when I was 14 because the economics demanded it.
I had a wonderful headmaster who kept an eye on me, quite frankly, until he died.
And I always likened him to Dr.
Arnold of Rugby, who was the great educationalist of Victorian times.
But I think Mr.
Kidd ?] was his equal.
And we all had tremendous respect for him.
And the same goes for our farmers.
It's-- it was an agricultural town.
And our farmers, in fact, employed most of the men and a lot of us boys.
From the age of 9, one got a job whenever there was a holiday.
And I was very fortunate enough to get a job with a family, a yeoman family.
And in fact, I'm still in touch with the family to this day, although I now live on the other side of the world.
Well, he was a professional soldier, until he did his time.
And then he developed with animals.
You must realize that there was a depression on, and lots of little farmers couldn't afford a veterinary surgeon.
And my father did a lot of veterinary work.
And Mother worked.
And we all worked, and we got by.
Oh, well, my older brother was Bill, who's dead.
He died as a result of war service.

Jack, my younger brother, who was very good at athletics-- in fact, he won the Victor Ludorum Cup in school-- he was killed in the Royal Air Force in Burma just as the war in the Far East was finishing.
And my sister married a soldier who was in the town during the war, and she still lives in the house where the family has lived ever since the house was built.
Yes, it's one of a number of houses built by the council before the-- round about 1930.
And of course, when Mrs.
Thatcher brought in the right to buy a council house, my sister bought it.
So now she owns the house where she's lived in, virtually, all her own life.
Oh, the-- it was always warm and friendly.
There was always something to eat.
We had very strict rules because my-- particularly, my mother was religious, but very kind.
But there were strict rules.
And if you broke the rules, then you-- you-- you learn not to break them again, until you forgot.
Now, it's very important that I get this across properly.

It was a little town, so although you might sort of be regarded as what we would say Church of England, if you preferred to go on the Sunday night to the Salvation Army, you went to the Salvation Army.
As long as you told your parents when you got home-- I didn't go to church tonight.
I went to Salvation Army with my mates-- all was forgiven.
But so we were Protestants.
In fact, I don't think there was a Roman Catholic family in the village or the small town.
And of course, when the war broke out, in--
No, I'm sure there wasn't.
Had there been one, I'm sure I would have remembered him.
But in actual fact, as soon as the war broke out, the Jewish school was evacuated from London area.
Just where, I don't know, because I was away with the war.
And they stayed in Chatteris for a long time.
And in fact, I think-- I'm not sure-- I think another one was evacuated there.
So by the end of the war, they had seen the Americans.
They'd seen the Canadians.
And seen a couple of Jewish schools in the town.
Well, I remember-- I remember the-- the kindness of everybody.
The place was kind.
Everybody was kind.
Mind, it didn't stop you getting your arse kicked.
But people were kind.
And my father was killed when I was about 15.
And I remember the man I was working for, the Charles family, who I've spoken to you about before, how kind he was to my mother.
So I would say that it was kind and law-abiding, and you had a lot of fun.
The rivers and everything, you could go wherever you like because you're country people.
You knew when to shut-- to shut the gate.
And you knew what you could do, and you knew what you couldn't do.
And since it was a small community, if you did something you shouldn't have, you can rest assured by the time you got home, your father knew.
Well, it was an accident at work.
And I'd rather not talk about it.
Oh, they-- from what I've already said, you will know that my headmaster made a great impression on me.
And I enjoyed every minute of it.
Because we-- it's so different to watch.
We had no grass playing field.
We had a small tarmacked area at the school, and that was our play-- playground.
And if we wanted to play football, we used to get-- which we did every week, of course-- we used to get teams together.
And we'd go and see a farmer.
And say, can we play football in your field next week?
And he'd either say yes or no.
But if he said no, he'd tell you why.
And he'd probably tell you to go and see somebody else, and it would be all right there.
So it was all very fun.
We used to play cricket.
We used to follow Test matches.
And Hobbs was our great cricketer.
I'm talking now at the days before Don Bradman, you know.
I remember him when he was knocking out at the runs over at The Oval.
Of course, we only saw it on the black and white news film.
There was no television as such.
And of course, radio was limited to crystal and small valve sets.
Well, by the time the newsreel got to our little town, it really wasn't news any longer.
It was history.
But they used to cover quite a lot of events, you know, the national events, and of course, the big international events.
But the sport, chiefly sport, the King, of course, anything to do with the King-- we were great monarchists.
And I still am, of course.
In spite of the philanderings of some members of the royal family, I'm still a great royalist.
Sorry?
Oh, I-- I honestly couldn't tell you.
But we had a theatre in the town.
But I'm afraid I didn't get mixed up with that.
The-- I'd surely go to the cinema and eat my packet of sweets.
But I really didn't go on theatre.
Oh, well, of course, see-- I remember which was a World War I film.
And I remember that was written by an Englishman.
And I remember another one, which was written by a German.
Remarque, I think his name was.
And then, of course, we had all the big biblical pictures, and those.
And then we had the westerns, Tom Mix-- the dog, Harold Lloyd, Charlie Chaplin.
There was always something to laugh about.
Well, every day, our headmaster would read to us bits out of the which was the And he would read parliamentary pieces to us and explain how Parliament worked.
This was unknown in the secondary school, but he did it routine.
We also read the tabloids, but not tabloids as you know them today.
The was a picture paper.
The wasn't as biased as it is today.
And the was a Beaverbrook newspaper.
Lord Beaverbrook became a great figure.
He was a Canadian.
He became a great figure as Minister of Aircraft Production in the dreadful days after Dunkirk.
Churchill pulled him in.
The Labour Party paper was the I don't remember reading very much in that except the adverts.
The of course, was a family newspaper, which was owned by the Berry family then.
And it was my good fortune during the war to serve with one of the Berrys for quite a long time when he was adjutant of my regiment, the 1st Mountain Regiment.
Michael, he's dead now, poor fellow.
But he was a very good chap.
Well, of course, it was just coming in.
You had the crystal set, where you adjusted the crystal, and you had your earphones or you shared your earphones with somebody else.
But anyway, you were-- there was no noise, because any a noise, you couldn't hear anything.
And then we both got the single valve, two valve, and three valve sets.
That-- it was great fun.
It was great fun.
My mother's favorite program used to be on a Sunday night.
Every Sunday night there used to be a program from St.
Martin-in-the-Fields.
And she would sit and listen.
And God help you if you made any noise while she was listening.
Oh, this would be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] late 20s, late 20s, at the very beginning of the 30s, late 20s.
But it was a very great evening service the-- from St.
Martin-in-the-Fields which was in Trafalgar Square.
Well, after I left school, we knew-- and the papers gave us news-- of the Japanese aggression in China, which was the first thing we saw.
And we knew of the discontent in Germany and Europe, which was the results of the second-- of the First World War and the Treaty of Versailles.
And the-- we watched with more than passing interest the rise of the struggle in Germany for power.
And-- but of course, the news we got was heavily filtered.
And above all, we had to earn our living.
So I'm afraid we didn't get involved in a lot of politics.
My MP at the time-- and I remember him coming to the village with Dolly, his wife-- was James de Rothschild.
And he lived in a place called Waddesdon Manor near Tring some miles to the south in-- I think in Buckinghamshire it is.
But we knew that things were bad, because by 1934, I decided I wanted to be a policeman.
And the only way to become a policeman was to join the army and to do a tour of duty in the army.
And if you did that and did well of it, it was almost note and take it into police, providing the police had vacancies.
And there was always police, and like the Metropolitan Police, which was reckoned to be the best police force in the world at that time.
And then, as now, they didn't carry arms, except on special mission.
[COUGHS]
Well, every boy got himself a job, as I told you before, because every penny counted.
And I got a-- I got employment with a family, a yeoman farming family, Charles family.
And every weekend, I used to go to work in the town houses, because there was a couple town houses.
Or if they needed me on the farm, I used to have to go on the farm.
And that meant getting up around about half past 5:00 in the morning, and then probably walking over three miles to work to be there at seven.
And you did work in the fields in the springtime, you know, singling sugar beet and hoeing.
And anything else to raise a cup of [INAUDIBLE], because that would provide your money to go and see the pictures.
See, there's method in one's madness.
And in the autumn, you would go picking up potatoes for the little small holders.
There are people who-- who after the First World War, the local authorities bought farms up and chopped them up into small holdings.
These people took these small holdings and made a living off on them.
But of course, the one thing they always tried to avoid was labor.
And on a Friday afternoon when you were leaving school, they would come to the school gate.
And they'd say, do you want a job potato picking tomorrow morning?
And you would say, yes, please.
And so you would get a job at something like that.
How much would they pay you?
Ah, well, very difficult to say, because the value of money is so different today.
I always thought that I was well paid and well treated whether I was working for the Charles family or whether I was working for a small holder.
Because eventually, I only worked for the Charles family.
There was somewhere for me to go every Saturday.
And I was virtually on the family's payroll.
What could you buy with a day's salary?
Well, if you smoke, which I didn't, you could buy five cigarettes for tuppence you might say you'd get 18 pence or a couple of bob if you were lucky-- 18 pence, more likely.
You could buy five woodbines for tuppence.
You could buy a packet of sweets for tuppence.
You could buy a penny bar of chocolate.
I'm talking about child purchases.
You could get a tup'ney seat at the cinema.
We used to call that the chicken run.
And you could get a padded seat for fourpence.
But of course, things were very tight.
So when you got home with your money, the first person's whose hand went into it was your mother's.
Because she might say, well, I need some money off you to help buy your next pair of shoes.
Things were-- things were tight.
But we never went hungry.
We never went ragged.
And we never went dirty.
I think the credit for that goes to my mother.
I think it was pretty normal.
Pretty normal, really-- you had set friends, you know.
Friends you went to chapel with or in the same class at school, same interests, which were invariably to do with the-- your countryside.
Bird nesting, of course, you'd get shot now if you went bird nesting.
That's very dark they've gone.
But we used to have bird's of each collections and wild flower collections.
And we used to have to record where we found our wild flowers, because records were kept at the school.
And if you found a very rare flower, well, you'd have to go and survey exactly-- exactly where it was.
And then you would give this detail to the school, and it was all recorded.
You know, it was pretty good really, you know, pretty good.
Because we had a railway engine used to go through-- we had a railway line to Cambridge.
That was, of course, the most powerful thing we ever saw.
It was then, of course, eventually, they started building a few aerodromes.
But by then, I had pushed off and joined the army.
But the railway locomotive was the most powerful thing we saw.
Sorry?
Well, you see, there were very few motor cars in the 20s.
Very few motor cars, Model T Fords, and a few models which have long since disappeared-- Swift's, [INAUDIBLE], Astons, Morris.
Motor bikes were generally belt driven.
They were certainly all kickstart, no electric start.
[INAUDIBLE]
Well, it was a permanent item on the school curriculum.
We used to have-- we used to have religious education first thing every morning when you went into school.
And we-- we had a morning hymn and an evening hymn.
And you'd sing-- sing them appropriately.
And it was up on the blackboard, written up for first week.
And you were expected to know it by the second week, because it came off.
And you knew that.
So you just learned it.
So you'd borrow your mother's hymn book when you get home and learn it, like learning a piece of poetry.
And then, of course, the chapels and the churches all had their own key families to support.
And you'd be enrolled with one, and you'd go to Sunday school.
In fact, you used to have to go three times on a Sunday.
I always thought that was a bit much.
But we always had freedom to go where you wanted to go.
And-- but as I told you earlier, you had to tell your mother when you got home.
I didn't-- haven't been at church.
I went to chapel.
Because you knew damn well that somebody who was in the church would miss you.
And would turn around and say, isn't Teddy well?
Oh, so you had to beat them to it.
But it was pretty practical, really.
Do you remember the name of that reverend?
Oh, yes, I do.
I remember the vicar's name was Bagshaw.
And [?

Fallsbury ?] Bagshaw, and the only name-- the only reason I remember it is because in the parish church-- which I have a photograph of which I'd like to show you-- there the pulpit was erected by and paid for by the vicar for his son who was killed in the First World War.
And I remember looking there every Sunday.
[INAUDIBLE] [?
Fallsbury ?] Bagshaw was-- gets ground in.
And then we had a congregational chapel, and the man was named Brewer.
And he had a white beard, so he was [INAUDIBLE] Brewer to all of us with his white beard.
But he was a nice chap and a very good preacher.
But I-- I don't remember the names of others.
But I remember them with affection though.
Well, of course, we covered the Old Testament.
And not just the highlights of it, but we really did cover it.
And I mean, there was a time when I could go right through Isaiah 40, and I didn't even need to look at the book.
But I should stumble a bit now, I think.
But the-- I cannot say that we were ever told anything which was anti-Jewish.
There was absolutely no anti-semitism whatsoever in the place, because we didn't have any.
It was rather like [INAUDIBLE].
We didn't have any little popes around the place.
No, the-- I first met Jews, oh, much later, in the army.
And when I-- particularly during the war, we got some of them that were called up.
And-- but they were chiefly Christian Jews.
Well, of course, we read written by Shakespeare.
And Shylock doesn't come out in that [LAUGHS] as a very generous character.
And so I'm afraid we probably got the wrong image.
We always liked listening to Mr.
Rothschild.
When he was our MP, he'd come down and talk to us.
Because we knew he was keen on racing, and somebody in the audience would always ask him for a tip for the derby or something like that.
And because that's what he liked, he played to that.
He's the only Jew who I can actually remember meeting in those very early days.
I remember his wife too, Dolly.
But I don't remember him actually talking about the Jewish problem in Europe.
I don't remember him talking about it.
He may well have done, mind you.
But I don't remember it.
A lot of water has gone under the bridge since I listened to Jimmy Rothschild.
